title: 第 9 節
en: Number of Hymns.

This collection, as we now possess it, handed down in the school of the >S'akalas, consists of 1017 hymns (Mantras or Suktas), while in the school of the Bashkalas their number amounted to 1025. There aro besides eleven hymns, called the Valakhilya hymns ^, which were added at the end of the sixth Anuvaka of the eighth Ma^u/ala. If we count them together with the 1017 hymns of the >S'akalas, we get a sum total of 1028 Vedic hymns. There are other spurious hymns called Khilas, but they are not counted with the hymns of the Samhita.
The Pratisakhyas.

These 1028 hymns became soon the subject of a most minute study, a kind of Masoretic exegesis. They had to be learnt by heart, and their exact pronunciation was laid down with the greatest care in works called Pratitfakhyas^. The date of these Pratisa- ^ There can be no doubt that these eleven hymns were added at a later time, and that they had existed before as a separate collection. This is best shown by the fact that they admit Galitas from themselves only, except in one doubtful case, tam tva vayam.

2 The Pratisakhyas form one of the six Vedangas, viz. the Siksha. Goldstiicker denied it. but he is refuted by the S'/k-prati.sakhya itself, which says, S. 827, that it is kritsna^/i vedangam anindyam 64 LECTURE IV.

khyas has been fixed with as much probability as is attainable in such matters, in about the fifth or sixth century B.C. They are certainly prior to the great grammarian F-Xnim who quotes verbatim from the Prati^akhya belonging to the /S'akala school of the Rig-veda ^ Date of the Pratisakhya.

In this Pratisakhya we have clear proof that the author of it, commonly called iS'aunaka, knew our collection of hymns, consisting of ten Ma^ic/alas. He speaks of dasatayi^ verses, i.e. verses found in the ten Ma7ic?alas. He actually quotes a passage as coming from the tenth Ma7?(/ala ^ (Sutra 313). In fact, his various rules presuppose not only the collection of the ten Ma72c/alas, but the exact collocation also of the hymns in each MaiicZala, such as we now possess them. It is thus and thus only that he is able to say, as he does, that a certain verse (I. 133, 6) is the longest, and another the shortest (VI. 45, 29), among all the verses of the ten Ma^i'ialas.

He goes even further, and he shows himself so certain of ever}^ consonant and vowel of the whole text of the ten Ma?zr/alas being in its right place, that he can say (S. 309) with perfect assurance and with arsham , * a complete Vedanga, faultless, and canonical.' The first Prati.'akhj^a published Avas that of the Eig-veda (1856-69). There are, lesides the two Pratisakhyas of the Yagrur-veda, one for the Vagasaneyi saw/hita, the other for the Taittiriya, and the Atharvapratisakhya. A Sama-pratisukhya has been published by Satyavrata Samasrami in the Usha, vol. i. No. 3 seq.
^ See Appendix V.

2 Rig-veda-pratisakhya, 997,9^^esh/M dasatayishu r?7ram,the longest of the verses among the Dasatayis. I thought that Dasatayi might here be meant as a name of ManrZala, because the text has rikXm, not rikshu. See, however, Sutras 946 and 993.
" The technical term Mandala occurs first in the Aitareya-arauyaka and iu the G?7hya-sutras.

SURVEY OF TEDTC LITERATURE. 65 perfect correctness, that, for instance, compounds ending with the words varu?ia and vrata shorten their last vowel, provided a consonant or semi-vowel follows, and this throui^h the whole of the Rio^-veda, except in thirteen hymns which are ascribed to Medhatithii (I. 12; I. 24).
Minutiae of tlie Pratisakliya.
Such statements occur again and again, and leave us in no doubt that not a single hymn could have been added to our collection, nor a single line be changed, after the date of the Pratisakhyas.

This is a most important point, for unless our arguments can be upset, we now possess the certainty that the Masoretic studies of the sixth and fifth centuries B. c. presuppose, nay postulate the existence, not only of Vedic hymns in general, but of our collection of these hymns in ten Ma7ic/alas ; and not only of our collection in ten Mar^c/alas, but of every hymn exactly in that place in which we now find it, with every word in its right place, nay with every vowel, either lengthened or shortened, exactly as they are lengthened or shortened in our MSS. This means that the text, exactly as we possess it in MSS. not more than about 500 years old, had become the subject of most minute scholastic studies about 500 B. c.
The Auukramaiiis of Sauuaka.

And now we may advance another step. The same author, >S'aunaka, to whom the authorship of one Pnitisakhya is ascribed, is also mentioned as the author of certain indices to the Rig-veda, called AnukramaTiis, literally, ' after-steppings.' Thtse indices ^ See Appendix VI. (2) F 66 LECTURE IV.
contain the number of Ma7?(ialas, of Anuvakas, and of hymns, the names of the authors and the deities, and the metres.

Most of these single indices have been preserved to us, or they existed at least as late as the time of Saya?za, fourteenth century. They were superseded, however, by the more comprehensive index of Katyayana, the SarvanukramaTii. These indices again presuppose the text of the ten Ma?itMas in all its important features exactly such as we now possess it, and thus enable us to say that the bridge of our argument spangi a distance of more than two thousand years, and lands us about 500 b. c. in the schools of the Brahmans. the so-called Parishads, where we see teacher and pupils learning by heart exactly the same Veda which we are studying at present.
Number of Verses of the Rigr-veda.

We saw that, according to the calculation of those ancient scholars, the Rig-veda-sa7>ihita consisted then, as it does now, of ten Ma'Jif/alas, eighty-five Anuvakas, and 1028 Suktas or hymns. But they went further in their calculations, and counted 10.40.2 verses^, 153,826 words, 432,000 syllables. These calculations, I am obliged to confess, have not yet been checked, except that of the verses, and here there is a discrepancy, but only a slight one. On an average, however, a hymn may be said to consist of ten verses, so that the number of 10,402 verses for 1028 hymns cannot be far wrong.
This will give you an idea of the extent of the real Veda, or the Rig-veda-sa^^ihita. If we take into ^ See Appendix VH.
SURVEY OF VEDIC LITERATURE. 67 account the length of the Vedic verses, as compared with the Greek hexameter, the Rig-veda may be said to contain nearly as much as the Iliad and Odyssey together.

This is all we have and ever shall have for studying that ancient period in the history of the Aryan race which precedes in language, mythology, and religion the Homeric period, hitherto the most ancient known period in the history of our race.
The Sama-veda.

If all the rest of what is called Vedic literature had been lost, we should not have been much the poorer for it. To the student of the history of Sanskrit literature the other so-called Vedas are no doubt of very high interest, as they form the connecting link between the ancient Vedic period and the later Sanskrit literature. But in the eyes of the general historian they cannot compare with what is really unique in the literature of the whole world, the hymns of the Rig-veda.
What then are the other so-called Vedas?

What is called the Sama-veda-sa')7?hita is no more than a compilation of verses contained in the Rig-veda, which had to be sung at certain sacrifices, and not simply to be recited, as were the hymns of the Rig-veda. Sam an means melody. Very often single verses are taken out of the hymn to which they originally belonged, in order to be sung together at certain sacrifices. There are only seventy- eight out of the 1549 verses of the Sama-veda^ which have not 1 See Ludwig, Rig-veda, iii. pp. 419-426. Aufrecht, Rig-veda, second edition, vol. ii. p. xlv.

been found in our text of the Rig-veda. All the rest are simply the same as we find them in the Rig-veda, with slight variations, representing the various readings of different recensions (b'akha), but by no means, as was once supposed ^, a more ancient text.
Ya^r-veda.
zh: The number of hymns contained in the Rig-Veda is generally stated at 1,028. They are distributed among ten books or mandalas (मण्डल), and they contain altogether about 10,580 stanzas or verses (ṛc, ऋच्). Some of these stanzas are repeated, and the actual number of different verses is therefore somewhat smaller. The whole collection is supposed to have been made at a very early period, and to have been preserved with the greatest care by oral tradition, from a time long anterior to the invention of writing, or at least to any written records that have come down to us. 梨俱吠陀所包含的頌歌數量通常被認為是1,028 首。這些頌歌分布於十部書籍或曼荼羅（मण्डल）中，總共包含約10,580 行詩節或經文（ṛc，ऋच्）。其中某些詩節有所重複，因此實際存在的不同經文數量稍微少一些。整個集合被認為是在非常早期編訂而成的，並透過口頭傳統以最大的謹慎保存下來，其時間遠早於文字的發明，或至少遠早於任何傳留至今的書面記錄。

這部典籍，據我們現今所有的版本，乃由沙卡羅（S'akalas）學派傳承下來，共有一千零一十七首讚歌（曼陀羅或蘇克塔），而在巴什卡羅（Bashkalas）學派中其數量則達到一千零二十五首。此外，還有十一首被稱為瓦羅基利亞讚歌（Valakhilya hymns）^，係附加於第八曼達羅（Ma^u/ala）第六阿奴瓦卡（Anuvaka）的末尾。若將其與沙卡羅學派的一千零一十七首讚歌合計，我們得到總共一千零二十八首吠陀讚歌。此外還有其他被稱為奇拉讚歌（Khilas）的偽典讚歌，但這些並未計入《吠陀本集》（Samhita）的讚歌之中。
《法緒莎卡耶》

這一千零二十八首讚歌很快就成為最細緻研究的對象，一種麻索拉傳統式（Masoretic）的註釋。它們必須被背誦記憶，其確切的發音方式在名為《吠陀音韻論》（Pratisakhya）的著作中被規定得最為謹慎。這些《吠陀音韻論》的年代^ ^ 無庸置疑，這十一首讚歌是在較晚的時期才被加入的，而它們之前曾作為獨立的文獻匯編而存在。最好的證明是它們只允許出現自己本身的《得利多經》（Galita），除了一個有疑義的情況——「tam tva vayam」。
《語音規範》（Pratisakhyas）構成六吠陀支之一，即《音韻學》（Siksha）。高德施圖克否認此說，但遭到《音韻語音規範》本身的反駁，該書第827節明確指出它是「kritsna vedangam anindyam」（完整無缺的吠陀支）。
已經以科學方法盡可能地確定為公元前第五或第六世紀。它們肯定早於大文法家跋笈多（Pāṇini），此人曾逐字引述屬於梨俱吠陀薩卡拉派（śākalā）的《對應經》（Pratisākhya）。^ 《對應經》的年代。

在這部《語音經》(Pratisakhya)中，我們擁有明確的證據，證明該部著作的作者（通常稱為商卡 (iS'aunaka)）認識我們由十部《曼陀羅》(Ma^ic/alas)組成的讚頌詩集。他提及「十部曼陀羅詩」(dasatayi^ verses)，即在十部《曼陀羅》中所見的詩句。他實際上引用了一段來自第十部《曼陀羅》的段落(第 313 經)。事實上，他的各種規則不僅預設了十部《曼陀羅》的匯編，也預設了如我們現今所擁有的那樣，每部《曼陀羅》中讚頌詩的精確編排。正因如此，他才能如他所言地指出，某一詩句(I. 133, 6)是十部《曼陀羅》中所有詩句中最長的，而另一首(VI. 45, 29)是最短的。

他甚至更進一步，他對於十部曼陀羅（Maṇḍala）全文中每一個子音與母音都處於其正確位置深感確信，因此他能夠（S. 309）以完全的把握和確定性說道「一部完整無缺的吠陀經分科（Vedanga），無可挑剔，且合乎正統」。首部出版的《保護論》（Pratiśākhya）是梨俱吠陀的版本（1856-69）。除了夜柔吠陀的兩部《保護論》外，分別為瓦迦薩奈亞集（Vagasaneyi saṃhita）和泰迪利亞本（Taittiriya）各一部，還有《阿闥婆保護論》（Atharvapratisakhya）。薩摩《保護論》由薩蒂亞瓦拉塔‧薩馬什拉米在《烏莎》（Usha）期刊第一卷第三號及其後續篇章中出版。
^ 參閱附錄五。
梨俱吠陀‧經文律（Rig-veda-pratisakhya）第 997,9 條：「esh/M dasatayishu r?7ram，在達薩塔衣派（Dasatayis）之中最長的詩句。」我原以為達薩塔衣派（Dasatayi）在此處可能被指為曼陀羅（ManrZala）的名稱，因為文本作 rikXm 而非 rikshu。然而參見第 946 條和第 993 條之規則。
「曼荼羅」（mandala）這個技術性術語最初出現在《愛達衍那奧義書》（Aitareya-aranyaka）和《家庭經》（Gṛhya-sutras）中。
完美的正確性，例如以 varuna 和 vrata 等詞結尾的複合詞，只要後面跟著輔音或半元音，就會縮短其末尾元音，這個規則貫穿整部《梨俱吠陀》，除了十三首歸屬於彌達提圖（Medhatithi）的讚歌外（I. 12; I. 24）。
《波羅底薩克利亞》(Pratisakliya)的細微之處
類似的陳述反覆出現，使我們毫無疑問地認識到，在《韻誦論》(Pratisakhyas)之後的年代，我們的收集本中不可能增添任何讚歌，也不可能改變任何一行。

這是一個最為重要的論點，因為若非我們的論證有誤，我們現在便確定地掌握了以下事實：第六與第五世紀前的馬索拉經文學研究，不僅預設了吠陀讚歌的普遍存在，更是確立了我們現有的十部曼陀羅（Maṇḍala）的讚歌集合；不僅如此，我們現有的十部曼陀羅的集合，乃至每一首讚歌都恰好位於我們如今所發現的位置，其中每個詞語都在其正確的位置上，甚且每一個元音，無論是長音或短音，都與我們的手稿中所標記的長短完全相同。這意味著，我們現有的手稿文本——其年代不超過約五百年——早已在約西元前五百年成為最為精密細緻的學術研究對象。
沙烏那卡的《經序》(Auukramaiiis)
而今我們可以更進一步。同一位作者‧舍烏那迦（S'aunaka），據說是某部《音韻經》（Pnitisakhya）的作者，同時也被提及為梨俱吠陀某些索引的作者，該索引稱為《後序》（AnukramaTiis），字面意思為「隨後的步驟」。這些索引^見附錄VI。(2)
包含摩羅闍（Ma7?(ialas）的數量、阿努瓦卡（Anuvakas）的數量、讚歌的數量、作者和神祇的名字，以及韻律。

這些單一的索引大多已被保留下來，或至少在十四世紀的沙亞那（Saya?za）時代仍然存在。然而，它們後來被迦膩衍那（Katyayana）更加全面的索引《薩婆奴克拉瑪尼》（SarvanukramaTii）所取代。這些索引又預設了十部曼尼沙（Ma?itMas）的文本在所有重要特徵上都恰好如我們現在所擁有的那樣，這樣使我們能夠說，我們論證的橋樑跨越了超過兩千年的距離，並將我們帶回到大約西元前五百年左右婆羅門的學派——所謂的派瑞沙德（Parishads），在那裡我們看到師生們用心記誦著完全相同的吠陀，正是我們現在正在研究的那部吠陀。
梨俱吠陀的詩頌數量

我們已經看到，根據那些古代學者的計算，梨俱吠陀集成（Rig-veda-sa1>ihita）當時如同現在一樣，由十個曼陀羅（Ma'Jif/alas）、八十五個阿奴瓦卡（Anuvakas）及一千零二十八首讚歌（Suktas）組成。但他們的計算更進一步，統計了一萬零四百零二個詩頌、十五萬三千八百二十六個字彙及四十三萬二千個音節。我必須坦白承認，這些計算迄今尚未經過驗證，除了詩頌數量之外，而在此方面確實存在出入，但只是輕微的差異。然而平均而言，一首讚歌可以說由十個詩頌組成，因此一千零二十八首讚歌對應一萬零四百零二個詩頌的數字應該不會有太大的出錯。
這將使你對真正的吠陀，或《梨俱吠陀‧本集》（Rig-veda-sa^^ihita）的範圍有所認識。如果我們取用^見附錄七。
吠陀文獻概覽。67 若考慮到吠陀詩行相較於希臘六步格詩的長度，梨俱吠陀所包含的內容可謂幾近於《伊利亞德》與《奧德賽》合計之量。
這就是我們現有的，以及我們永遠都將擁有的全部資料，用以研究雅利安種族歷史中那個久遠的時期——在語言、神話和宗教上都先於荷馬時代的時期，而荷馬時代迄今為止乃是我們種族歷史中最為久遠的已知時期。
薩摩吠陀（Sama-veda）
倘若所謂吠陀文獻中的其他部分全部遺失，我們的損失並不會很大。對於梵文文獻史的研究者而言，其他所謂的吠陀固然具有極高的興趣，因為它們構成了古代吠陀時期與後世梵文文獻之間的連接環節。然而在一般歷史學家的眼中，它們無法與整個世界文獻中真正獨一無二的東西相比──梨俱吠陀的讚歌。
那麼，其他所謂的吠陀又是什麼呢？
所謂《娑摩吠陀梵本集》（Sama-veda-sa'hita）不過是《梨俱吠陀》中所收詩句的彙編，這些詩句必須在某些祭祀中被唱誦，而不像《梨俱吠陀》的讚美詩那樣只是被念誦。「娑摩」（Sam an）意為旋律。通常單獨的詩句會從它原本所屬的讚美詩中被取出，以便在某些祭祀中被唱誦在一起。在《娑摩吠陀》的一五四九首詩句中，只有七十八首沒有
在我們的梨俱吠陀文本中已經發現。其餘的都完全相同於我們在梨俱吠陀中所見的，只有輕微的變異，代表不同誦本傳統（b'akha）的各種異讀，但絕非如曾經所認為的^，更古老的文本。
梨俱吠陀（Yajur-Veda）。
